local_names.romanized_local: Pol Sambol (Sinhala romanization); Theengai Sambal (Tamil romanization)

food_subgroup: Fresh coconut relish with Maldive fish and chilli; served as a condiment accompaniment (standard serving ~60g / 2 tablespoons); HIGH sodium (~550mg per 60g serving)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Pol Sambol. Closest parent would be a coconut-based condiment or relish. URI left null pending FoodOn curation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sri Lankan fresh coconut relish with Maldive fish (pol sambol) — closest_parent match within FoodEx2 condiment/relish hierarchy
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GDE is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a coconut-based condiment/relish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Pol Sambol specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Pol Sambol or equivalent Sri Lankan coconut relish.

serving.description: 2 tablespoons (~60g) — standard accompaniment with rice, hoppers, or string hoppers
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Pol Sambol is approximately 60g (2 tablespoons), typically served alongside rice and curry, hoppers, or string hoppers at a Sri Lankan meal.

nutrient_source.title: Sri Lanka Food Composition Tables — Medical Research Institute (MRI) FCP 2012; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for Maldive fish (dried skipjack tuna) sodium component calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Pol Sambol macros and micronutrients derived from Sri Lanka MRI FCP 2012 coconut and condiment entries. Sodium value cross-validated using USDA SR Legacy dried skipjack tuna data (3,500–4,500mg Na per 100g dried fish) and component-level calculation for a standard Pol Sambol recipe (approximately 60–65% fresh coconut, 8–10% Maldive fish, remainder chilli, onion, lime, salt). Per-100g sodium of 917mg is consistent with this composition. All other macros consistent with MRI FCP coconut relish entries.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of coconut-based condiments — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fresh coconut and low-carbohydrate relishes; peer-reviewed GI methodology applied
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pol Sambol in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~20 is estimated from the closest analogous foods: fresh grated coconut (GI ~45 per Sydney GI Database, but fat-dominant composition suppresses glycaemic response) and the very low available carbohydrate content (~8g per 100g, ~4.8g per 60g serving). GL per 60g serving = (20 × 4.8) / 100 = ~1.0. The high fat content from coconut (predominantly lauric acid) further attenuates postprandial glucose response. This estimate is consistent with the low-GI classification for high-fat, low-carbohydrate condiments.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium density — ~550mg sodium per 60g serving (917mg per 100g), almost entirely from Maldive fish (dried salted skipjack tuna)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients eating Pol Sambol 2–3 times daily may consume 1,100–1,650mg sodium from this condiment alone; the single most effective dietary intervention is requesting or preparing Pol Sambol with reduced or omitted Maldive fish, which can reduce sodium by ~275mg per serving (half Maldive fish) or to ~50mg per serving (vegan/no-fish version).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic impact — GI ~20, GL ~1.0 per 60g serving; high fat from coconut lauric acid does not raise blood glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pol Sambol is glycaemically safe as a condiment for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (GL ~1.0 per serving); the primary clinical concern is sodium from Maldive fish rather than glycaemic load, and patients should be counselled to reduce Maldive fish content if also managing hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12.5g per 100g; ~7.5g per 60g serving, predominantly coconut lauric acid) combined with high sodium (~550mg per 60g serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For patients with cardiovascular disease, the dual burden of high saturated fat (predominantly lauric acid from coconut, whose cardiovascular effect is debated but not neutral) and high sodium from Maldive fish warrants portion moderation and preference for reduced-Maldive-fish preparations; limiting to one small serving per day and requesting less Maldive fish are the most practical interventions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (lauric acid from coconut) and high sodium potentially interacting with corticosteroid therapy
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis on corticosteroid therapy should be aware that the high sodium content (~550mg per 60g serving) may exacerbate corticosteroid-induced fluid retention; lauric acid's inflammatory impact is debated but the sodium burden is the primary concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pol Sambol (පොල් සම්බෝල in Sinhala; தேங்காய் சம்பல் / Theengai Sambal in Tamil) is Sri Lanka's most ubiquitous condiment, served at virtually every Sri Lankan meal — with rice and curry, with hoppers (appa), with string hoppers (idiyappam), and with roti. It is a no-cook, hand-ground relish made from freshly grated coconut mixed with ground Maldive fish (dried salted skipjack tuna — the Sri Lankan equivalent of Japanese katsuobushi), dried red chilli flakes, finely sliced red onion, fresh lime juice, and salt. The texture is coarse and moist; the flavour profile is intensely savoury-umami from the Maldive fish, fiery from the chilli, bright and acidic from the lime, and rich from the fresh coconut. Per 100g: ~195 kcal, 6.5g protein, 15g fat (predominantly saturated lauric acid from coconut), 8g carbohydrate, 917mg sodium. Per standard 60g serving (2 tablespoons): ~117 kcal, ~4g protein, ~9g fat, ~5g carbohydrate, ~550mg sodium. GI ~20 (low), GL ~1.0 per serving — glycaemically negligible. The defining clinical concern is sodium: the Maldive fish contributes 3,500–4,500mg Na per 100g dried fish, making Pol Sambol one of the highest-sodium condiments in Sri Lankan cuisine by serving volume. Patients eating 2–3 servings per day may consume 1,100–1,650mg sodium from Pol Sambol alone. For hypertensive patients and those with cardiovascular disease or diabetic nephropathy, reducing or omitting Maldive fish is the single most impactful Sri Lankan dietary sodium intervention. A vegan version (no Maldive fish) reduces sodium to approximately 50mg per serving. Source: Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables 2012 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation.
common_user_queries: how much sodium in pol sambol

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from Sri Lanka MRI Food Composition Tables 2012 (an approved regional source) supplemented by USDA SR Legacy component calculation for Maldive fish sodium. The MRI FCP is a credible national food composition database but is not as extensively validated as USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Sodium value is well-supported by component calculation. GI is an analogous estimate (no direct measurement in University of Sydney GI Database). Overall confidence: medium (input record stated 0.7 confidence; this is consistent with medium).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Pol Sambol not available in Sri Lanka MRI FCP 2012 or USDA SR Legacy at the composite dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite relish not reported in MRI FCP 2012. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for a fresh coconut and fish relish with no hydrogenated fats; consistent with USDA SR Legacy coconut and fish entries. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to 0.0: confirmed negligible per MRI FCP 2012 — no significant carotenoid-containing ingredients in standard Pol Sambol. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed negligible — fresh coconut and Maldive fish in this preparation do not contribute meaningful vitamin D per MRI FCP 2012. GI value of 20 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database closest-match entries for fresh coconut and high-fat low-carbohydrate condiments; no direct GI measurement for Pol Sambol exists in the Sydney GI Database as of 2026. This is a Sri Lankan dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore Chinese, Malay, and Hindi multilingual name requirements (Singaporean dish rules) do not apply. Tamil name is included as Tamil is one of Sri Lanka's two official languages and Pol Sambol has a well-established Tamil name (Theengai Sambal). Sinhala script form included in aliases.
